rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: Project home page is orphaned: add it to the sidebar and make the DDx logo a link
description: ## Observed The project home page (`/nodes/.../projects//`) renders a useful overview — queue summary (ready/blocked/in-progress) plus three action cards (drain queue, re-align specs, run checks). User feedback: "I see it when I navigate to a new project, then it disappears." ## Root cause `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/NavShell.svelte:26-36` — the sidebar's `pages` array contains: beads, documents, graph, workers, sessions, personas, plugins, commits, efficacy. **There is no entry for the project home/overview.** Once a user clicks any sidebar link, there is no nav-level path back to the home page — only clicking the project in the `ProjectPicker` again, or editing the URL. Adjacent: the `DDx` brand in the header (`NavShell.svelte:61`) is not a link — common affordance for "go home" is missing. ## Proposed direction 1. Prepend a "Overview" (or "Home") entry to `pages` in `NavShell.svelte`, routing to `/nodes//projects//` (empty path segment after the project id). Use the `LayoutDashboard` icon that's already imported, or pick a dedicated home icon — design call. 2. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. Pick one and stay consistent. Trivial change; the biggest decision is whether the brand link goes to node-root or project-home. Recommend project-home when a project is selected (less jarring) and node-root otherwise.
acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer on any project sub-page, I can always return to the project overview with a single click — either from a sidebar entry or from the DDx brand in the header.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. Sidebar has an "Overview" (or equivalent) entry at the top of the `pages` list, routing to `/nodes/<nodeId>/projects/<projectId>/`.
2. The entry is the active/highlighted one when the current URL matches the project-home route exactly (no trailing segment after `projectId`).
3. DDx brand in the header is an anchor. When a project is selected, it routes to that project's home; otherwise routes to `/`.
4. Playwright: navigate into `/beads`, click the Overview sidebar entry, assert URL is project home and queue summary renders. Then navigate into `/sessions`, click the DDx brand, assert URL is project home.
5. No regression to any existing sidebar entry.

2. The entry is the active/highlighted one when the current URL matches the project-home route exactly (no trailing segment after `projectId`).
3. DDx brand in the header is an anchor. When a project is selected, it routes to that project's home; otherwise routes to `/`.
4. Playwright: navigate into `/beads`, click the Overview sidebar entry, assert URL is project home and queue summary renders. Then navigate into `/sessions`, click the DDx brand, assert URL is project home.
5. No regression to any existing sidebar entry.

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: Move session-log persistence fully behind service execution
description:
cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go currently decodes public service events and recreates internal session start/end records using internal/core and internal/session. That gives the CLI private knowledge of the session-log schema and duplicates lifecycle persistence logic that should be owned by the service. CONTRACT-003 now says SessionLogDir chooses storage location but session-log persistence is service-owned.

Implement that ownership in code. Native and subprocess execution should write the authoritative lifecycle and terminal records from inside the service execution path, including cancellation/error/final attribution. The CLI should consume the public event stream and returned session-log path only; it must not synthesize session-start/session-end records or depend on internal session-log payload types. Preserve enough information for replay, usage aggregation, and execute-bead evidence bundles.

In-scope files:
- cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go
- service_execute.go and any session-log helpers it depends on
- session-log/replay/usage tests affected by the ownership move
- execute-bead/native logging regression tests such as agent-b9bd700f where appropriate

Out of scope:
- Broader benchmark trace persistence improvements beyond what is needed to make service-owned logs authoritative
- CLI route/upstream-service cleanup outside session-log ownership
acceptance:
1. Service-owned execution writes the authoritative session lifecycle records; cmd/agent no longer recreates session start/end records from decoded service events.
2. Final results still expose session-log path and replay/usage flows continue to work against service-owned logs.
3. Targeted tests pass: go test ./cmd/agent ./service* ./session/... -run "Test(Session|Replay|Usage|Execute).*"

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: integration: limit doctor --plugins to plugin installs
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cli/cmd/doctor.go:452
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: `checkInstalledPlugins` iterates every row in installed state without filtering by package type, even though `InstalledEntry` records resource installs as well as plugins. A minimal repro with an installed `resource` entry (`persona/example`) makes `ddx doctor --plugins` emit `/tmp/.../persona.md/package.yaml: open ...: not a directory` and `missing package.yaml` for a non-plugin file. This breaks the plugin audit for users who have installed single resources.
Suggested fix: Restrict `ddx doctor --plugins` to package types that actually represent plugin roots (at least `plugin`, and `workflow` if workflows are intentionally audited), and skip `resource` entries entirely.
acceptance: Given installed.yaml contains both plugin and resource entries, when `ddx doctor --plugins` runs, then it emits plugin_validation findings only for plugin-like entries and no false `package.yaml` findings for resources

rationale: closed focused bead with small acceptance surface
title: Record route-target-route attribution in results, sessions, and telemetry
description: Update result/session/telemetry surfaces so the embedded runtime exposes DDx-consumable routing facts without leaking inner upstream-service-candidate tables. Record requested route-target input, requested route-target_ref when present, route key, selected upstream-service, resolved concrete route-target, attempted upstream-services, and failover count in Result, session start/end records, and OTel-aligned telemetry. Preserve existing upstream-service/cost attribution semantics. Do not require DDx to understand route-target_routes or candidate priority state; DDx only needs intent plus outcome attribution so it can combine embedded-runner evidence with its own cross-runner routing data. Background: DDx SD-015 explicitly says DDx must not duplicate embedded upstream-service selection, but it does need the embedded runtime to surface the upstream-service actually chosen.
acceptance: Result, session artifacts, and telemetry include requested route-target/ref, route key, selected upstream-service, resolved route-target, attempt chain, and failover count; existing usage/cost semantics remain correct; DDx can consume attribution fields without reading upstream-service candidate config; tests cover successful routing, failover, and direct-upstream-service override paths.

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: test(service): tests calling agent.New() leak user config — break verify against base revisions
description:
Several service_*_test.go tests call agent.New(...) with default options. agent.New defaults to loading the user's \$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/agent/config.yaml. Any upstream-service type added to the user's config that the agent module under test doesn't recognize causes those tests to fail with 'unknown upstream-service type'.

Today's beadbench preflight run (agent-beadbench-preflight against agent-openrouter-qwen36plus, 2026-04-28) hit this: the bead executes correctly through the route-target loop, but the verify gate runs 'go test ./...' against the bead's verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (pre-lucebox). That older code rejects 'type: lucebox' / 'type: vllm' from the user's live config.

Failing tests captured (sample):

  service_upstream-services_test.go:680 TestNewWaitsBrieflyForInvalidQuotaRefresh
  service_upstream-services_test.go:721 TestNewStartupQuotaRefreshContinuesAfterTimeout
  service_upstream-services_test.go:765 TestPrimaryQuotaRefreshWorkerRefreshesOnTimer
  service_validation_test.go:15 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessRoute-targetBeforeRun
  service_validation_test.go:43 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessReasoningBeforeRun
  service_runner_dispatch_test.go:41,90,119 TestExecute_Dispatches*
  service_profiles_test.go:13,61,112 TestServiceProfiles_*

All same root cause: agent.New() loads the live user config without test isolation.

## Fix paths

1. Tests that don't actually need the user's config should pass an explicit ServiceConfig stub (no upstream-services, or a known shape).
2. Or, set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a temp dir in test setup so agent.New() loads an empty config.
3. Or, add an option to agent.New that skips XDG lookup.

Pick whichever is most consistent with how other test-isolation patterns work in this repo.

## Why P2

Doesn't block normal CI (CI clean container has no user config). Does affect any verify-gate run on a base revision predating a upstream-service-type addition — a bead correctness signal we care about during parity measurements.
acceptance:
1. service_*_test.go tests calling agent.New() with default options either set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a t.TempDir() or pass an explicit ServiceConfig that doesn't depend on the user's live config.
2. 'go test ./...' from a verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (or any commit predating lucebox/vllm) passes regardless of what the runtime user has in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml.

title: Add gitignore management to ddx init for runtime and upstream-service-local state
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: repo bootstrap hygiene for DDx-managed and upstream-service-local runtime state. Evidence covers current execute-bead worktree churn under .ddx/, missing ignore coverage for beads.lock and .execute-bead-wt-* paths, and the absence of any gitignore management in ddx init.
</context-digest>
Teach `ddx init` to manage ignore rules for local DDx runtime artifacts and upstream-service-local configuration/state that should not be committed.

## Scope
- Define the canonical ignore entries DDx should add or maintain during init
- Cover DDx runtime artifacts like execute-bead worktrees and lock state
- Cover local upstream-service state/config directories that DDx may create or depend on during agent execution and that should remain untracked
- Keep tracked project state intact: do not hide durable files like .ddx/beads.jsonl, .ddx/config.yaml, or checked-in skill/docs content
- Make the init behavior idempotent and safe for existing .gitignore files
- Add tests covering insertion and non-duplication of managed ignore entries
acceptance: ddx init adds or updates documented .gitignore entries for DDx runtime artifacts and upstream-service-local state that should remain untracked; the behavior is idempotent and does not mask durable tracked DDx files; upstream-service-local config/state directories created by agent execution are ignored by default; and automated coverage verifies ignore-file insertion and deduplication

title: Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead
description:
There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Runner, WorkerRecord.Route-target, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.

This bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.

## Required changes

### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
Register alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:
  --json         emit raw JSON array
  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C
  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)

### 2. Data sources (two paths)
A. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.
B. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:
   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)
   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, runner, route-target, started_at

### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan
For each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:
  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)
  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)
  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available
  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields

### 4. Table output (default)
Columns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | RUNNER | ROUTE-TARGET | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS
- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)
- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)
- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID

### 5. Merge and deduplicate
When both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()
- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)
acceptance: ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, RUNNER, ROUTE-TARGET, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.

title: Phase 5: move cli/internal/agent/{compare,benchmark,quorum,condense}.go upstream into agent (then delete from DDx)
description:
Move four comparison/benchmark files upstream into agent module — they ARE the comparison-suite infrastructure the integration suite (agent-fd22bfeb) builds on.

Files (with tests):
- compare.go + compare_test.go (comparison runner, calls into routing engine)
- benchmark.go + benchmark_test.go (benchmark runner)
- quorum.go (quorum strategies for comparison)
- condense.go (output condensation)

Once landed in agent: DDx files delete; ddx agent compare command (if it exists) calls upstream via Service or via a dedicated bench API method (or stays in DDx if it is bead-orchestration not LLM-routing — depends on what compare.go actually does, decide during the move).

Pre-condition: routing infrastructure already moved upstream (compare.go calls RankCandidates which is now agent-internal).
acceptance:
- [ ] compare.go/benchmark.go/quorum.go/condense.go and their tests live in agent (path TBD, likely internal/comparison/ or similar)
- [ ] DDx versions deleted
- [ ] Existing compare/benchmark functionality still callable (via agent CLI or DDx Service-thin wrapper)

title: Add session continuity across Run calls
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>go fmt ./... before every commit · go test ./... and go test -race ./... for concurrent code · golangci-lint run · gosec ./... · govulncheck ./... · log with slog, wrap errors with context, and keep tests table-driven with testify</practices>
<governing>PRD P1 — add an explicit continuity API or request field so conversation history can carry across multiple `Run()` calls without changing the default single-run behavior; review hx-0b94868b keeps it in the ready execution queue.</governing>
</context-digest>

Discovered by repo alignment review issue agent-e0a5d4b7. Implement the planned ability to carry conversation history across multiple agent.Run calls within a session, with an explicit API for passing prior context and tests that verify continuity without breaking the default single-run behavior.
acceptance: The runtime exposes an explicit session-continuity API or request field, prior conversation history can be carried across Run calls, default behavior remains unchanged, and tests cover history carry-forward semantics

rationale: closed focused bead with small acceptance surface
title: feat: compaction user-message tail re-inclusion after compact
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Summary Injection — the compaction summary replaces all compacted messages. SD-006 §Codex research — keeps up to 20K tokens of recent user messages alongside summary. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-06 — compaction no-fit paths must fail closed.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently compaction/compaction.go replaces compacted history with only the summary message. Codex retains the most recent real user messages (up to a token budget) alongside the summary so the route-target retains actual request history.

What to change in compaction/compaction.go (or the Compactor function):
- After generating the summary, collect real user-role messages from the tail of the pre-compaction history (exclude system prefix messages, previous compaction summaries, and tool result messages — only real user messages).
- Accumulate messages from newest to oldest until a configurable token budget is exhausted (default: 20000 tokens, chars/4 heuristic). Per SD-006 §Overlong individual user messages, truncate individual messages that exceed the budget rather than dropping them.
- The replacement history becomes: [summary_message, ...tail_user_messages] ordered so the summary is first and user messages follow in chronological order.
- Expose the budget as a new field UserMessageTailTokens int in CompactionConfig (default 20000; 0 = disabled).
- All existing compaction tests must still pass.
- Add test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion verifying tail messages appear in replacement history.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.
acceptance: go test ./... passes; CompactionConfig gains UserMessageTailTokens field (default 20000); new test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion exists and verifies that after compaction the replacement history contains both the summary message and the most recent real user messages up to the token budget, in chronological order

title: Implement picompat package (pi config readers)
description:
Implement pi config readers per SD-007 tasks 1-3.
Required files:
- picompat/auth.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/auth.json → map[upstream-service]credential
- picompat/route-targets.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/route-targets.json → map[upstream-service]upstream-serviceDef
- picompat/settings.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/settings.json → default upstream-service/route-target
- picompat/translate.go: merges auth+route-targets+settings → []config.Upstream-serviceConfig

Pi upstream-service name → agent mapping per SD-007:
- anthropic → anthropic (type: anthropic)
- openai-codex → openai (type: openai-compat, default URL)
- openrouter → openrouter (type: openai-compat)
- Custom from route-targets.json → mapped from api field

Two-source merge algorithm per SD-007:
1. Start with route-targets.json upstream-services (have baseUrl and route-target IDs)
2. Look up matching auth.json credentials
3. For auth-only entries, use well-known defaults
4. Apply settings.json defaultUpstream-service/defaultRoute-target

Skipped with warnings per SD-007:
- !command API key values
- Unsupported api field values
- headers with !command resolution

Output uses SD-005 field names exactly.
acceptance: picompat/auth.go, picompat/route-targets.go, picompat/settings.go, picompat/translate.go implemented; unit tests compile

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: Finalize the service-boundary specs for CLI, service, and upstream-service ownership
description:
The architecture review established the target doctrine: cmd/agent is a first-party consumer of the public DdxAgent service contract; service owns config-backed routing, upstream-service construction, native failover, event normalization, and session-log persistence; upstream-service adapters and internal/core stay behind the service boundary. The current docs were stale and partly permissive: CONTRACT-003 described cmd/ddx-agent as a transitional adapter with an internal-import allowlist; SD-002 still said the CLI constructs upstream-services and calls agent.Run(); architecture.md described the older agent.Run/upstream-service-package shape; SD-005 still described upstream-service construction in terms of passing one concrete Upstream-service to agent.Run().

Finalize those docs so they state the correct architecture precisely and consistently. Explicitly document that PreResolved is only for RouteDecision values produced by ResolveRoute, not CLI-reconstructed candidate lists; document that SessionLogDir controls storage location but session-log persistence is service-owned; document that callers never receive upstream-service instances.

In-scope files:
- docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md
- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-002-standalone-cli.md
- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-upstream-service-config.md
- docs/helix/02-design/architecture.md
- contributor guidance if needed to state the boundary clearly

Out of scope:
- Code changes beyond the minimum needed to keep docs/tests aligned
- Deleting CLI routing/session-log code (separate child beads)
acceptance:
1. CONTRACT-003 states that cmd/agent is a service consumer only, forbids CLI-side upstream-service construction and session-log synthesis, and constrains PreResolved to service-produced decisions.
2. SD-002, SD-005, and architecture.md describe the same ownership route-target without references to CLI-side agent.Run()/upstream-service construction.
3. Documentation checks and targeted tests pass: go test ./cmd/agent -run "TestCLI(ServiceContractUsesTypedEventDecoder|MainDoesNotImportOrCallInternalCoreRun|InternalImportBoundaryAllowlist)"

rationale: closed focused bead with small acceptance surface
title: Implement deterministic script runner for orchestration stress testing
description:
The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every "fake" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.

The consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.

## What this bead builds

A new deterministic `script` runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):

- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go
- Always available (no binary probe required)
- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Route-target or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)
- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations
- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe

## Directive grammar

Line-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:

```
append-line <path> <text>          # append text to file (create if missing)
create-file <path> <content>       # create or overwrite file
delete-file <path>                 # rm -f path
modify-line <path> <regex> <repl>  # sed-style in-place substitution
run <shell-command>                # exec in WorkDir with clean env
commit <message>                   # git add -A && git commit -m <message>
set-exit <code>                    # final exit code (default 0)
no-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0
fail-during <index>                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive
sleep-ms <n>                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)
```

Directives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:

```
append-line tests/smoke.txt "hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
commit "smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
```

## Invocation

From the CLI: `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`

Programmatically from a test:
```go
runner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Runner: "script"})
result, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{
    WorkDir: wtPath,
    Route-target:   "testdata/append-smoke.txt",
    Correlation: map[string]string{"bead_id": beadID, "attempt_id": attemptID},
})
```

## Implementation notes

- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted
- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent
- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)
- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests
- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo

## What this enables

Every test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this runner existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.
acceptance: New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The runner is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=<file>` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn
description:
<context-digest>
Observed while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.
</context-digest>
Tighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.

## Scope
- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn
- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default
- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state
- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary
acceptance: Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.

title: runner-test: build golden-master integration suite for real runneres
description:
Build the integration-level evidence suite for the runner migration. The suite must prove the service can drive each supported live runner and can replay recorded interactions deterministically. This is the answer to whether wrapping Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Pi, and opencode is actually supported and tested.

Required runneres for this bead: claude, codex, pi, opencode. Gemini may be included if the service dispatch bead wires it and the capability matrix marks it supported.

Required behaviors to test per runner when supported by its capability matrix:
- run a prompt through Service.Execute
- list or explicitly report route-target support/no-discovery semantics
- pass route-target, reasoning level, permissions, workdir/context, timeout, and metadata
- capture progress events and final normalized text
- capture token usage when the runner exposes it
- capture quota/status data for claude and codex when the capability matrix marks quota supported
- replay the same interaction sequence identically from cassette without credentials

Record mode must be opt-in and must fail fast when credentials, subscription, binary, configured route-target, transport dependency, or quota permissions are missing. Replay mode must be runnable in normal CI without live credentials. Ad-hoc fake binaries are acceptable only for shared parser/process infrastructure; they must not be counted as live runner integration evidence.
acceptance:
1. Integration tests are organized around the public capability matrix: every supported required capability is exercised for every required runner, and unsupported optional capabilities are asserted as unavailable.
2. Record mode can run against live claude, codex, pi, and opencode, fails fast with clear diagnostics when authentication or binaries are missing, and records cassettes with scrubbed environment/credential data.
3. Replay mode runs deterministically without credentials and verifies event order, progress events, final text, usage where available, quota/status where available, routing metadata, and session log metadata.
4. Tests cover route-target selection, configured reasoning level, workdir/context behavior, timeout/idle handling, and metadata propagation through Service.Execute.
5. The suite distinguishes reusable deterministic runner tests from real live-runner evidence and does not claim ad-hoc fake-binary tests as live integration coverage.
6. Documentation explains how to run record mode and replay mode.
7. go test -tags=integration ./... passes in replay mode without live credentials.
8. Record-mode commands for each live runner are documented and fail fast when credentials are missing.

rationale: closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal
title: integration: preserve upstream-service identity fallback for upstream-services without ChatStartMetadata
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:182
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new chat-span bootstrap path always sources ddx.upstream-service.system from chatStartIdentity. When a upstream-service does not implement ChatStartMetadata, chatStartIdentity returns "unknown" instead of preserving the existing session-level upstream-service identity. Before this commit, StartChat seeded Upstream-serviceSystem from sessionUpstream-service, so failed calls from upstream-services that only implement SessionStartMetadata still retained a meaningful identity. This change regresses those error spans to "unknown". The repo already has such a upstream-service in upstream-service/virtual/virtual.go, which exposes SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata.
Suggested fix: Make chatStartIdentity fall back to sessionStartIdentity(upstream-service) for upstream-serviceSystem when ChatStartMetadata is unavailable, while still leaving server address and port unset unless the richer metadata hook is implemented.
acceptance: When a upstream-service implements SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata and Chat returns an error, the ended chat span records ddx.upstream-service.system with that upstream-service identity instead of unknown, while OpenAI-compatible upstream-services still record resolved server.address and server.port.

title: correctness: summary since window must ignore late-updated pre-cutoff beads
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cli/internal/processmetrics/processmetrics.go:285
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new summary window helper uses bead `updated_at` as the inclusion gate, so a bead whose close fact and only correlated sessions are both before the cutoff still appears in `ddx metrics summary --since ...` if it was edited later for unrelated reasons. `beadsVisibleInSummaryWindow` admits that bead, and the downstream summary logic counts it in `summary.Beads.Total/Closed`, `summary.Beads.UnknownCost`, `summary.Cost.Beads`, and `summary.Cost.Features` even though there are no in-window lifecycle or session facts. Repro: with one bead closed on 2026-01-01, one correlated session on 2026-01-01, and only `updated_at=2026-03-05`, `Summary(Query{Since: 2026-02-01, HasSince: true})` returns `beads.total=1 closed=1 unknown_cost=1 cost.beads=1` while sessions, cycle-time, and rework are all zero. SD-016 says `--since` means include only facts observed on or after the cutoff for the relevant fact type; a tracker metadata edit after the window is not a new lifecycle or cost fact.
Suggested fix: Define summary bead inclusion from the same fact timelines used by the underlying sections, rather than raw `updated_at`. A bead with no in-window close/reopen/session facts should not surface in the windowed summary or its cost/feature counts, and regression coverage should include the late-update/no-facts case.
acceptance: Given a bead whose first close and only correlated sessions are before the cutoff but whose updated_at is later, metrics summary --since excludes it from bead totals and cost/feature unknown counts; a regression test covers the late-update/no-facts case; cd cli && go test ./internal/processmetrics -count=1 passes

title: execute-bead: audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent upstream-service calls
description:
## Root cause RC4 from ddx-0a651925

After RC1 is fixed (caller ctx propagates), upstream-service HTTP calls still need per-request deadlines. A stalled TCP socket that delivers headers and then stops sending body bytes — particularly during streaming token reads — can hang a goroutine for days.

Grepping the `DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14` module cache shows short timeouts only on discovery probes (`upstream-service/openai/discovery.go:46,174`, `openai.go:123`). The main `Run` call path inside agent upstream-services appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).

## Scope

1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` upstream-services in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.
2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each upstream-service call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.
3. For streaming bodies, enforce an **idle-read limit** on the response body so a mid-stream stall cannot hold a goroutine indefinitely (e.g. `http.Transport.ResponseHeaderTimeout` + periodic `SetReadDeadline` on the underlying conn).
4. Surface upstream-service-timeout failures as a distinct status in `ExecuteBeadResult.Detail`.

## Non-goals

- Rewriting upstream-services; minimally invasive change only.
- Changing DDx-side timeout defaults (RC2 covers outer bound).

## Prerequisite

Sibling RC1 bead must be resolved first, otherwise adding deadlines inside upstream-services is useless while DDx still feeds them `context.Background()`.
acceptance:
- [ ] Audit report (checked-in .md or bead-comment) lists every `http.Client.Do`-equivalent call in agent upstream-services with its current deadline semantics
- [ ] Each main upstream-service call path installs a per-request deadline (default 15m or catalog-configurable)
- [ ] Streaming body reads terminate within a bounded idle period (ReadDeadline or equivalent)
- [ ] Integration test: fake HTTP server accepts the connection, sends headers, then stops responding. Upstream-service call returns within deadline with a distinct error

title: workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty
description:
## Observed

User clicks "Drain queue" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/<id>/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.

Verified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.

```
$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json
{
  "id": "worker-20260422T205846-f23f",
  "kind": "execute-loop",
  "state": "running",
  "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",
  ...
}
```

So the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:

```go
if projectID != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {
    continue
}
```

- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.
- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.

These are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.

The global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `""` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.

Note: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.

## Proposed direction

Two options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.

### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site

```go
expectedPath := ""
if projectID != "" {
    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)
    if !ok {
        return nil // unknown project id → no workers
    }
    expectedPath = proj.Path
}
...
if expectedPath != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {
    continue
}
```

### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record

Add `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).

Recommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.

## Scope note

This is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.

## Out of scope

- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).
- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.
acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.

2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**
   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).
   - Start one worker for each project.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-1>)` returns exactly worker 1.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-2>)` returns exactly worker 2.
   - `workers` (global) returns both.
   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.

3. **Regression test — backward compat.**
   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.

4. **End-to-end validation.**
   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.
   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.

5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**
   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.
   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.
   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.

rationale: no_changes/review text says bead needs splitting
title: test: migrate TestExecuteBeadNoMerge to real git + script runner
description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadNoMerge() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script runner.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptRunnerRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadNoMerge$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.
acceptance: 1) TestExecuteBeadNoMerge no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptRunnerRepo (or equivalent real-git runner). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadNoMerge$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.

- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadNoMerge$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.

rationale: three or more attempts without a closing commit
title: Implement execute-loop-first help, skill, and operator guidance
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: consumer-facing execute-bead / execute-loop guidance. Evidence covers the shipped CLI help surfaces, the open docs gaps tracked by ddx-a4195a8e and ddx-7a01ba6c, and the need to teach humans and agents one coherent route-target: execute-bead is the primitive, execute-loop is the normal queue-driven surface.
</context-digest>
Implement the first complete consumer-facing guidance surface for DDx agent execution.

## Required changes
1. Update CLI help text and any nearby command docs so `ddx agent execute-bead` is described as the single-bead primitive and `ddx agent execute-loop` is described as the queue/supervisor command built on top of it.
2. Add or update user-facing operator docs and CLI reference surfaces so users discover `execute-loop` first for normal queue-driven work, with concrete examples for:
   - `ddx agent execute-loop`
   - `ddx agent execute-loop --once`
   - `ddx agent execute-loop --poll-interval <duration>`
   - `ddx agent execute-bead <bead-id>` for bounded/manual runs
3. Update the ddx-bead skill and any relevant internal skill/operator guidance so agents are told explicitly:
   - planning/document-only beads are valid execution targets
   - execute-loop is the default queue-driven entrypoint
   - execute-bead is the lower-level primitive for targeted/manual execution
4. Document the result/close semantics operators need in practice:
   - which outcomes/statuses close a bead
   - which outcomes preserve and leave the bead open
   - which JSON fields automation should read (`status`, `outcome`, `preserve_ref`, `result_rev`, `session_id`)
5. Make the operator guidance consistent with the current merge route-target:
   - merge is the default successful outcome
   - explicit required execution documents are the automatic merge gates
   - `--no-merge` is the manual preserve override

## Boundaries
- Do not invent a new bead shape or merge-gate field
- Use the existing execute-bead / execute-loop / execution-document contract
- Keep the user-facing guidance aligned with the actual shipped command behavior and documented result surface
acceptance: CLI help, operator docs, and ddx-bead skill guidance all teach the same route-target: execute-bead is the primitive, execute-loop is the normal queue-driven surface, planning/document-only beads are valid execution targets, merge is default unless explicit required execution docs block landing or --no-merge is set, and the documented JSON/result semantics tell operators and automation how close/preserve outcomes behave

rationale: no_changes/review text says bead needs splitting
title: exec: tier-based auto-escalation driven by resource discovery
description:
The CLI should not require the user to enumerate runner/route-target pairs. The tool already knows which tiers exist (route-target-catalog.yaml: cheap/standard/smart) and which runneres are configured. Given a live probe of each upstream-service's health at attempt time, the loop can auto-escalate on its own.

## Principle

Discovery over configuration. The user shouldn't write a fallback chain — the tool should figure it out.

## Default policy (no flags)

- Start at **cheap** tier, execute the bead
- If merge succeeds AND post-review (gap #1) APPROVES → close
- If anything fails (execution_failed, no_changes after N retries, post_run_check_failed, REQUEST_CHANGES from review) → escalate one tier and retry
- Chain: cheap → standard → smart → exhausted (preserve, flag for human)
- Review agent runs at max(impl_tier+1, smart). Never review cheap with cheap.

## Resource discovery at attempt time

- Before picking a concrete runner/route-target for a tier, probe each candidate upstream-service (local inference host, API key validity, network reachability)
- If upstream-service returns 502 / 500 / timeout / auth-rejected within a short budget, mark it unhealthy with a cooldown and try the next upstream-service in the tier
- If every upstream-service in a tier is unhealthy, skip the tier and escalate
- Per-upstream-service health state is process-local and visible via a new 'ddx agent upstream-services' sub-command (ddx-09ea1884 Visibility 3)

## Rare overrides

- --min-tier standard (skip cheap — useful for hard beads known to need it)
- --max-tier standard (never reach smart — cost control)
- --runner <name> / --route-target <name> (debug override; disables escalation)

## Surface

Default invocation becomes:

  ddx agent execute-loop --once

Everything else is implicit. Review, escalation, and resource discovery are on-by-default.

## Events

Each attempt appends an execute-bead event: { runner, route-target, tier, upstream-service, probe_result, attempt_status, review_status }. The event stream shows exactly why a tier/upstream-service was picked or skipped.

## Context

Observed during axon ADR-018 loop:
- bragi was down (502) — I manually probed with curl and switched to vidar-coder. Tool should do this.
- qwen3-coder-next merged but opus review rejected — I manually re-invoked with sonnet. Tool should escalate automatically.
- Resulting CLI invocation in my notes was too complex (—-fallback-chain a/b,c/d,e/f --post-review g/h). User feedback: too many knobs. Reach for a default that just works.

## Acceptance criteria replaced with discovery-driven ones (see accept field)
acceptance:
- [ ] execute-loop with zero flags escalates across cheap → standard → smart on failure
- [ ] Review agent auto-selects a tier >= impl tier, defaulting to smart
- [ ] Upstream-service health probe runs at attempt time; unhealthy upstream-services skip cleanly with cooldown
- [ ] Each attempt records tier + upstream-service + probe_result + outcome on the bead
- [ ] Integration test: cheap upstream-service 502 + standard succeeds → bead closes with escalation trail visible
- [ ] --min-tier / --max-tier overrides work and are documented
- [ ] --runner / --route-target debug overrides disable escalation
- [ ] Zero-flag 'ddx agent execute-loop' is the documented happy path

rationale: three or more attempts without a closing commit
title: work: ready epics are silently skipped by execution loop
description:
In the agent repo on 2026-04-20, `ddx bead ready --json` reported three ready open beads: two epics and one task. The task (`agent-05d81638`) was under retry cooldown until 2026-04-20T06:08:22Z. Running `ddx work --once` started a worker (`worker-20260420T004727-aff9`) that exited with 0 attempts and no log output. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.

This is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. If epics are intentionally container-only, the default ready surface or the work-loop output should make that distinction explicit. If epics are supposed to be executable planning/decomposition targets, the work loop should handle them rather than dropping them.

Expected behavior: when `ddx work` skips ready epics, the operator gets a clear reason and next action, e.g. "0 execution-ready beads; skipped 2 epics because epics require child task decomposition; 1 task blocked by retry cooldown until <timestamp>". Alternatively, `ddx bead ready` should default to execution-ready or visibly mark non-execution-eligible epics so it does not imply `ddx work` can process them.
acceptance:
1. A regression test covers a tracker state with at least one open ready epic and no execution-eligible task beads; `ddx work --once` does not silently exit with an empty log/status.
2. The operator-facing output or JSON result explains why ready epics were skipped and includes the count/IDs or issue type reason.
3. `ddx bead ready` and `ddx bead ready --execution` semantics are documented in CLI help or status output so users can predict what `ddx work` will process.
4. Existing queue/work tests pass.

rationale: repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope
title: telemetry: emit override and rejected_override events from Execute
description:
Bead 1 of ADR-006. Emit override and rejected_override events from Execute so the routing-quality signal becomes measurable.

When ExecuteRequest has any of Runner, Upstream-service, or Route-target set, the service:
1. Runs ResolveRoute as today, producing the constrained decision (the dispatched candidate).
2. Runs a second ResolveRoute with the override axes stripped, producing the unconstrained auto decision.
3. Emits an 'override' event before the final event with the payload shape from ADR-006 §3 (user_pin, auto_decision, axes_overridden, match_per_axis, auto_score, auto_components, prompt_features, reason_hint, outcome).
4. Outcome is populated post-execution from the existing final-event status fields.

When a pin fails pre-dispatch (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service, runner/upstream-service incompatibility), emit a 'rejected_override' event with the same shape minus outcome.

prompt_features.estimated_tokens uses ServiceExecuteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens if provided, else best-effort runner-level tokenizer (nullable).

reason_hint accepts a free-form string from Metadata["override.reason"] (programmatic) or a future --override-reason CLI flag.

In-scope files:
- service.go (event payload struct definitions)
- service_events.go (event type constants + decoded event mapping)
- service_execute.go (override capture + event emission, second ResolveRoute call)
- service_execute_test.go (event-shape and emission-order tests)

Out of scope:
- RouteStatus / UsageReport metric exposure (bead 2)
- CLI surface (bead 3)
- Any change to existing routing-decision events
- Automatic learning loop
acceptance:
1. ExecuteRequest with no override axes set produces no override event (verified in TestExecuteEmitsNoOverrideEventForUnpinnedRequest).
2. ExecuteRequest with Runner, Upstream-service, or Route-target set produces exactly one override event before the final event. Verified in TestExecuteEmitsOverrideEventBeforeFinal across all three axes individually and in combination.
3. The override event's auto_decision field is computed from a second ResolveRoute call with the override axes stripped. When pinning matches what unconstrained auto would produce, match_per_axis values are true; the event still fires (coincidental-agreement case). Verified in TestOverrideEventCoincidentalAgreement.
4. axes_overridden lists exactly the axes the caller pinned (subset of {runner, upstream-service, route-target}). Verified in TestOverrideEventAxesOverriddenIsExplicit.
5. Outcome is populated post-execution with status, cost_usd, duration_ms matching the final event's values. Verified in TestOverrideEventOutcomePopulatedFromFinal.
6. Pre-dispatch failure of a pin (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service) produces a rejected_override event and no override event. Verified in TestRejectedOverrideEventOnOrphanRoute-target.
7. prompt_features.estimated_tokens reflects ServiceExecuteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens when set; nullable otherwise. Verified in TestOverrideEventPromptFeaturesPopulation.
8. reason_hint reflects Metadata["override.reason"] if set; empty string otherwise.
9. go test ./... -count=1 passes.

rationale: three or more attempts without a closing commit
title: Detect execute-bead worktree edits even when the agent makes no commits
description: A Codex execute-loop run on ddx-28c15f0e updated FEAT-002, SD-019, and website server docs inside the managed worktree, but DDx classified the attempt as no_changes because it only looked for agent-created commits. Execute-bead must treat a dirty worktree with tracked edits as real output, preserve or land it appropriately, and record accurate result status instead of discarding useful work.
acceptance: execute-bead and execute-loop no longer report no_changes when the worktree contains tracked file modifications without agent-created commits; the attempt preserves or lands the changes and result artifacts reflect the actual diff state

title: test: migrate TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorMessageInOutput to real git + script runner
description:
Migrate cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorMessageInOutput() from fakeExecuteBeadGit + fakeAgentRunner to real git + script runner.

Pattern:
- Use cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go newScriptRunnerRepo(t, beadCount) to build an isolated repo with real git.
- Use runGitInteg (from cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go) for any supplemental git invocations, so GIT_* env is scrubbed.
- Replace fakeExecuteBeadGit with the RealLandingGitOps flow used in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go.
- Replace fakeAgentRunner with the script runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go) driven by a per-attempt directive file. When the test needs to simulate 'agent emits no commits' or 'agent emits a dirty worktree', encode that as script directives rather than a canned Result struct.
- Preserve every assertion — the point is that the test now exercises real git + real merge/preserve semantics, not that the surface shrinks.

Scope limit: ONLY this test. Do not migrate any other test in the same file; touching another test expands blast radius outside the bead.

Verification: cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorMessageInOutput$' -count=1 passes. No struct ending in -GitOps or -Runner that fakes the thing under test is referenced from this test after the migration.

Governing concern: concerns.md §testing 'no mocks, period' + 'never mock the thing you are testing'. Parent: ddx-d9df348d.
acceptance: 1) TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorMessageInOutput no longer references fakeExecuteBeadGit or fakeAgentRunner. 2) Test dispatches through RealLandingGitOps + newScriptRunnerRepo (or equivalent real-git runner). 3) cd cli && go test ./cmd -run '^TestExecuteBeadAgentErrorMessageInOutput$' -count=1 green. 4) No other test in agent_execute_bead_test.go is modified.

rationale: no_changes/review text says bead needs splitting
title: Align specs for prompt templates, tracked execution artifacts, and commit provenance
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: governing contract for execute-bead prompts and execution evidence. Evidence covers the current FEAT-006/API-001 workflow, the prompt fallback bug, the desire to keep beads authored and relatively stable, and the new requirement that execution evidence be tracked in git so autoresearch can replay, compare, and analyze runs.
</context-digest>
Align the governing docs around one execute-bead evidence contract.

## Goals
- Define the execute-bead prompt template as a DDx-owned rationalizer over bead fields plus resolved governing references, not a bead mutation and not a speculative summary of whole specs
- Define the stable tracked artifact set for each execution attempt: prompt, manifest, result, checks, normalized log, and usage/upstream-service pointers
- Define the split between tracked execution evidence and ignored local scratch/runtime state
- Define the default commit policy for successful and preserved attempts
- Define the rule that all programmatically-added commit message metadata must be rendered from tracked machine-readable files
acceptance: FEAT-006, FEAT-012, FEAT-014, FEAT-015, and API-001 describe the same contract: execute-bead compiles a deterministic prompt from bead data plus resolved references; each attempt produces tracked machine-readable execution artifacts; ignored runtime scratch is clearly separated from tracked evidence; and commit-message metadata is projected from tracked files rather than ad hoc runtime state

rationale: repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope
title: Bead detail GraphQL resolver scans every project and every bead on each request
description:
## Observed

Opening a bead in the sidepane on `/nodes/.../projects/.../beads?q=test` takes 5+ seconds. The beads list renders quickly; the delay is on single-bead lookup.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver.go:93-100` — the `Bead(id)` resolver calls `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots("", "", "", "")` with no filters, which at `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105` walks **every project**, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (full directory scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), then iterates the result in Go to find one matching ID.

So every sidepane open = `O(projects × beads_per_project)` disk reads + parses, for a lookup that should be `O(1)` or `O(log n)`.

`bead.Store` already exposes `Get(id)` (`cli/internal/bead/store.go:450`), but the resolver doesn't use it — nor does it know which project the bead lives in, because the GraphQL schema exposes `bead(id:)` without a project scope.

## Proposed direction (not prescriptive)

Any of these would resolve it; pick based on measurements:

1. **Project scope the query.** Change schema to `bead(projectId:, id:)` (or accept a composite global id) and call `Store.Get` directly.
2. **Maintain an in-memory index** `beadID → projectID` refreshed on bead mutations and on a debounced filesystem watcher; resolver does one map lookup + one `Store.Get`.
3. **Short-TTL snapshot cache** behind `GetBeadSnapshots`, invalidated on bead writes.

Before picking: add a baseline measurement runner (see acceptance) so we know the actual p50/p95 before and after, not just anecdote.
acceptance:
**User story:** As a developer browsing the web UI beads view, I want clicking a bead to open its detail pane within ~200ms on a repo with hundreds of beads across several projects, so the UI feels interactive.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. A benchmark/runner exists (Go benchmark or scripted GraphQL client) that measures end-to-end `bead(id:)` latency against a fixture repo with ≥ 5 projects and ≥ 500 beads total. Output is a p50/p95 number, not a pass/fail bool.
2. Baseline is recorded in the bead notes (actual number measured on the current `main` binary at the time of fix).
3. After the fix, p95 for `bead(id:)` on the fixture is ≤ 50ms in-process / ≤ 200ms over HTTP on a dev laptop.
4. The fix does not introduce a stale-read regression: writing a bead via `ddx bead update` and then fetching it via GraphQL returns the new value on the next request (verified by a test that does write-then-read).
5. Concurrency-safe: an integration test spawns N concurrent `bead(id:)` queries interleaved with `bead update` mutations and asserts no panics, no torn reads, and consistent responses.
6. Playwright e2e: open `/nodes/.../beads`, click a bead row, assert the sidepane shows the bead title within 1s (generous budget; guards against regressions not the p95).

rationale: no_changes/review text says bead needs splitting
title: Remove upstream-service-native parsers and GlobalUpstream-serviceHealth shadow (atomic)
description:
Land agent-b896b406 (upstream-service-native parser removal) + agent-bcd2ff47 (GlobalUpstream-serviceHealth removal) together. The atomic-pair framing is from the parent bead ddx-7bc0c8d5: splitting creates a window where operator surfaces (doctor, /api/upstream-services, MCP tools, agent usage) read from different sources than execute-loop, so both land in one PR.

## Files to delete / rewrite

- cli/internal/agent/session_evidence.go (357 LOC): Codex native session JSONL, Claude stats/cache, OpenRouter /auth/key, LM Studio /v1/route-targets.
- cli/internal/agent/routing_signals.go (~450 LOC Runner-method variant; loadCodex/Claude/LMStudio helpers).
- cli/internal/agent/routing_signal_adapters.go, routing_signal_http.go.
- cli/internal/agent/claude_quota_cache.go — entire ReadClaudeQuotaSnapshot / WriteClaudeQuotaSnapshot / resolveClaudeQuotaSnapshotPath subsystem.
- cli/internal/agent/state.go — ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision and ClaudeQuotaDecision type.
- cli/internal/escalation/GlobalUpstream-serviceHealth singleton + Upstream-serviceHealthTracker.

## Call sites to migrate

- workers.go:511 (IsHealthy) / :593 (Mark)
- agent_cmd.go:1702 (IsHealthy) / :1791 (Mark)
- agent_cmd.go:680 (routing_signal.CurrentQuota.State == "blocked")
- agent_cmd.go:704 (ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision)

## Migration target

- svc.ListRunneres — RunnerInfo.Quota / UsageWindows / Account
- svc.ListUpstream-services — Upstream-serviceInfo.EndpointStatus
- svc.RouteStatus — RouteStatusReport with RouteCandidateStatus.Healthy
- svc.RecordRouteAttempt — RouteAttempt with status/reason

Vocabulary translation shim in buildUpstream-serviceSummary / buildUpstream-serviceDetail maps current "fresh/stale" to upstream's "ok/stale/unavailable" (consumer-side mapping only; not an upstream API change).

## Observable behavior changes (consumer surfaces)

- ddx agent doctor --routing --json: state.claude_quota_decision field disappears.
- ddx agent doctor --routing human text: claude-quota-cache line disappears.
- ProbeRunnerState via state.go.
- ddx agent usage enrichment.
- REST /api/upstream-services.
- MCP ddx_upstream-service_list / ddx_upstream-service_show.

Not affected: SvelteKit frontend upstream-services page uses GraphQL upstream-serviceStatuses only (no cache fields).
acceptance:
(1) session_evidence.go, routing_signals.go, routing_signal_adapters.go, routing_signal_http.go, claude_quota_cache.go deleted.
(2) state.go: ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision and ClaudeQuotaDecision removed.
(3) cli/internal/escalation: GlobalUpstream-serviceHealth singleton and Upstream-serviceHealthTracker removed.
(4) workers.go, agent_cmd.go call sites migrated to svc.ListRunneres / svc.ListUpstream-services / svc.RouteStatus / svc.RecordRouteAttempt.
(5) buildUpstream-serviceSummary / buildUpstream-serviceDetail perform fresh/stale → ok/stale/unavailable translation.
(6) go -C cli build ./... and go -C cli test ./... green.
(7) ddx agent doctor --routing and /api/upstream-services exercised manually; behavior matches upstream RouteStatusReport semantics.

rationale: three or more attempts without a closing commit
title: Adopt XML-tagged execute-bead prompt template
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead prompt-template structure. Evidence covers the shipped synthesized prompt in cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go, the new tracked execution bundle under .ddx/executions/, and the need for a more deterministic machine-readable prompt contract for replay, diffing, and future autoresearch.
</context-digest>
Evolve the synthesized execute-bead prompt from markdown-heading sections to an explicit XML-tagged structure.

## Goals
- Replace markdown section headings in the synthesized execute-bead prompt with XML-style tags for machine-significant structure
- Preserve the current human-readable content while making the prompt easier to parse, diff, and validate deterministically
- Define which sections are required, optional, and repeatable in the prompt template
- Keep the prompt aligned with the tracked execution manifest and future commit-provenance rendering
- Update the governing specs and contracts so the prompt-template contract is documented rather than only implied by code

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006 and any applicable execute-bead contract docs to define the prompt-template structure
- Align the execution-evidence lane docs so prompt.md is explicitly structured, machine-readable evidence

## Required implementation work
- Update prompt synthesis in cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go to emit XML-tagged sections
- Update automated coverage to assert the new tagged structure and prevent markdown-header regression
acceptance: FEAT-006 and the execute-bead contract docs define an XML-tagged prompt template for execute-bead; cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go emits that tagged structure into prompt.md; and tests verify the required tags and reject regression to markdown-heading-only prompt structure

title: cmd/work: migrate ddx work CLI dispatch to LoadAndResolve + RunWithConfig + behavioral e2e
description:
Stage 1 of SD-024. Migrates the CLI dispatch site at cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1997 to use config.LoadAndResolve + ExecuteBeadWorker.RunWithConfig instead of constructing ExecuteBeadLoopOptions from CLI flags. This bead unblocks FEAT-022 G's CLI path: ReviewMaxRetries from .ddx/config.yaml will actually drive review-retry behavior on 'ddx work' invocations.

- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (around line 1997 — runAgentExecuteLoop)

- Server worker dispatch (bead 8)
- GraphQL StartWorker dispatch (bead 9)

title: [artifact-run-arch] update prd.md for artifact + 3-layer architecture
description:
Per plan: docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md.

Scope of PRD update:

(1) Restructure Problem section into 6 clusters keyed to thesis principles in product-vision.md:
- Abstraction (no structure / no composition / no integrity guarantees / no transferability)
- Iteration over tracked work (no reusable work-item store / no reusable execution evidence)
- Methodology plurality (no reusable agent dispatch — every workflow tool reinvents)
- LLM physics (NEW cluster — token cost as a permanent constraint; nothing in current PRD)
- Evidence/provenance (no provenance for generated artifacts; no measurement; no feedback capture)
- Human-AI collaboration (no composition for handoffs; no discoverability; no network access)

Existing problem bullets re-grouped under these headers; add the LLM-physics cluster.

(2) Goals (primary): reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP goal.

(3) Non-goals: sharpen loop wording — DDx owns mechanical queue drain (ddx work); content-aware supervisory decisions remain plugin/HELIX. Add: 'DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers.'

(4) Feature blurbs: FEAT-001 (CLI: run/try/work top-level + ddx agent passthrough); FEAT-005 (multi-media identity); FEAT-006 (layer-1 consumer + structural passthrough); FEAT-007 (sidecar + generated_by + separate staleness); FEAT-008/021 (media-type rendering + regenerate + layer-aware run views); FEAT-010 (3-layer architecture + substrate unification); FEAT-019 (child of FEAT-010 — evaluation UX, workflow shapes -> skills).

Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate; invocation upstream.

Do NOT touch docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md.

rationale: three or more attempts without a closing commit
title: Define server and UI surfaces for epic execution
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: server and UI epic execution surfaces. Evidence covers the host+user server plan, worker list requirements, queue readiness UI, and the need to show epic workers separately from ordinary single-ticket execute-loop workers.
</context-digest>
Define the server/API/UI surfaces for epic execution.

## Goals
- Define the separate epic lane in queue views
- Define epic worker records, branch/worktree visibility, and child-progress visibility
- Define how users inspect epic merge-gate results and the final merge candidate
- Keep single-ticket workers and epic workers distinct in the server route-target
acceptance: FEAT-008 and SD-019 define a separate epic lane, epic worker visibility, branch/worktree inspection, child-progress visibility, and epic merge-gate inspection distinct from ordinary single-ticket execute-loop surfaces

title: agent: migrate internal helpers off RunOptions/CompareOptions/QuorumOptions
description:
Stage 2 prereq for B22 (SCOPE EXPANDED 2026-04-26 per worker analysis on attempt 1).

Migrate ALL ddx-side callers off the three legacy options structs to AgentRunRuntime + ResolvedConfig. After this bead lands, the legacy types in cli/internal/agent/types.go are unreachable from any caller — B22 can then trivially delete them.

In-scope (expanded from original 10-file list):
- cli/internal/agent/ helpers: virtual.go, script.go, claude_stream.go, agent_runner.go, agent_runner_service.go, route-targets.go, grade.go, service_run.go, session_index.go, routing_metrics.go, compare_adapter.go, runner.go
- cli/cmd/ callers: agent_cmd.go (RunViaService → RunWithConfigViaService)
- cli/internal/agent/ dispatch sites: execute_bead.go (dispatchAgentRun), execute_bead_review.go (dispatchReviewRun)
- All *_test.go files that construct RunOptions{}/CompareOptions{}/QuorumOptions{} composite literals

Original 10-file scope was internally inconsistent (worker found that migrating a subset would either leave intermediate states the runtimelint gate fires on, or require touching out-of-scope callers anyway). Expanded scope authorizes the worker to perform the full migration in a single atomic change.

Out-of-scope (still):
- The three type DEFINITIONS at types.go:36/81/171 (B22 final deletes these)
- Any change unrelated to this options-struct migration
acceptance: field RunOptions removed from cli/internal/agent/grade.go. field RunOptions removed from cli/internal/agent/service_run.go. field RunOptions removed from cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go. field CompareOptions removed from cli/internal/agent/grade.go. field QuorumOptions removed from cli/internal/agent/grade.go. cd cli && go build ./... passes. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... passes.

rationale: no_changes/review text says bead needs splitting
title: execute-bead: agents make edits but skip git commit; iteration commits land only execution evidence
description:
Observed today across 5 attempts on the axon repo (claude/sonnet, claude/opus, codex/gpt-5.4-mini): the agent does real work in the worktree (multiple Edit tool calls touching real source files) but never runs git commit. Result: iteration commit is built by ddx-land-coordinator with ONLY execution-evidence files (.ddx/executions/<id>/result.json, manifest.json, usage.json), no actual code or doc changes. The post-merge codex reviewer correctly flags BLOCK with rationale 'change set only adds execution metadata files'.

Concrete evidence (axon repo, 2026-04-29):

  Attempt: 20260429T235353-d14f6b91 (axon-80979cb8 / A1 sub-bead)
  Runner: claude / route-target: sonnet
  Duration: 817 sec, cost $3.61, 132 tool calls
  Tool breakdown: 79 Bash, 34 Read, 11 Grep, 8 Edit
  Edit targets: ui/src/lib/api.ts, ui/src/lib/policy-evaluator.ts,
    ui/src/routes/.../policies/+page.svelte, ui/tests/e2e/link-policy-retry.spec.ts
  Iteration commit (ddx-land-coordinator): contains exactly 2 files —
    .ddx/executions/20260429T235353-d14f6b91/result.json (23 lines)
    .ddx/executions/20260429T235353-d14f6b91/usage.json (9 lines)
  Net: zero actual edits landed despite $3.61 of route-target time spent making them.

The same pattern appeared on:
  20260429T235406-20eeaf2c (axon-db7a6d0a, claude/sonnet, $0.57, 4.2 min, BLOCK)
  20260429T190657-fa91f49b (axon-ab2e52e0, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.08, 24s, stalled)
  20260429T190634-579df880 (axon-80979cb8, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.12, 104s, BLOCK)
  20260429T190646-5aa5b46e (axon-db7a6d0a, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.06, 25s, BLOCK)

Total burn today: ~$4.50 across 5 attempts on 3 beads, 0 closed.

The execute-bead prompt template tells the agent to commit:
  '4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with git add <specific-paths>...'

So agent self-commit is the contract. But:

(a) When typecheck/lint/test fails, the agent is told to fix or write no_changes_rationale.txt — but apparently many agents are bailing silently without writing the rationale either, so the runner sees a clean worktree (no diff staged or committed) and treats it as 'agent made no commits'. The iteration then carries only the auto-generated execution evidence.

(b) There may also be a path where the agent commits but a pre-commit hook silently rejects it. Have not confirmed; the worktree was reaped before I could inspect.

The issue spans multiple runneres (claude, codex via embedded openrouter), so it is not specific to one upstream-service's commit semantics. Either:
  - The runner should reject 'agent made no commits AND no no_changes_rationale.txt' as a separate failure class instead of silently letting ddx-land-coordinator land an empty iteration
  - The agent prompt should make rationale-writing on incomplete work non-optional with stronger language
  - The runner should run a final-stage check 'did the agent produce ANY signal — commit OR rationale — and if not, treat as runner-execution-failed and surface to the operator'
acceptance:
AC1. When an execute-bead session exits without committing in the worktree AND without writing .ddx/executions/<id>/no_changes_rationale.txt, the runner records a NEW dedicated status (e.g. 'agent_silent_exit' or 'no_evidence_produced') instead of letting ddx-land-coordinator land an iteration containing only execution evidence.

AC2. The codex reviewer's BLOCK rationale ('change set only adds execution metadata files') becomes redundant — the runner catches this case before the reviewer is even invoked, saving a redundant review pass and its cost.

AC3. The execute-bead prompt template is updated so the rationale-on-incomplete-work instruction is harder to skip — ideally a tool the agent must invoke (e.g. SubmitNoChangesRationale tool) rather than 'write a file and exit'.

AC4. When the agent did run Edit tool calls but the worktree has no commit, the runner logs a warning naming each file the agent edited that is now uncommitted, so operators can recover the work or diagnose the silent-commit-failure.

AC5. Documentation in docs/ explains the agent-self-commit contract, the rationale-file fallback, and the new 'agent_silent_exit' classification.
